chapters.title: A new idea that could reshape work
chapters.text:
Flexible work arrangements, also called flextime, is a concept that has gained ground over the past decade. It covers any work setup outside the usual 40 hour week. Common examples include telecommuting, part time work, remote work, and condensed workweeks. The closest cousin to a four day week is part time work, where workers trade fewer days for lower pay. The four day week is fundamentally different. Workers put in only four days a week but earn 100 percent of their salary, provided they can hit 100 percent of the productivity their role demands.

It sounds strange, yet giving workers an extra day off each week has made several companies more productive.

Andrew Barnes came up with the idea on a flight after reading an issue of The Economist. The issue reported two pieces of productivity research done in Canada and the United Kingdom. The studies showed that office workers were productive for only about 1.5 to 2.5 hours out of the eight they were expected to work. Andrew formed a hypothesis that giving workers a free day each week would boost engagement, which would in turn raise productivity and profit. It was just a hypothesis, and he needed a way to test it.

His company, Perpetual Guardian, became the test subject for the new idea. He looked for business leaders who had tried anything similar in the past but could not find any. He knew there was a lot of work ahead.

Andrew wanted to test the hypothesis in real conditions. He got sign off from the management team and then shared the idea with his employees. Two outside researchers were brought in to make the trial more solid.

They ran the trial for two months. The results were striking. Perpetual Guardian has now made the four day week a permanent part of its policy.

News outlets in New Zealand picked up on the success of the experiment and interviewed Andrew about his idea. In social media terms, the concept went viral. Today it is being discussed in 75 countries, and several governments are weighing whether to make it law. Whether you are new to the four day week or already familiar with it, this summary will walk you through the advantages for businesses and society, how to introduce it in your own organization, and the basic pitfalls to avoid so that it works for you.

chapters.title: Work stress is costing everyone
chapters.text:
If we freeze frame the world of work as it stands today, we see a few clear patterns. Workers are more distracted than ever. Mental health problems tied to work stress are on the rise. People put in long hours without producing results that match the time spent, and companies lose money because of it.

In a 2017 survey of nearly 2,000 full time office workers, 79 percent of employees said they were not fully engaged during their 37 to 40 hour work week. In fact, 54 percent admitted that they looked forward to office distractions like chatting with coworkers, making personal phone calls, and browsing news sites during the day.

When work feels like the main source of stress, people pull back from it.

If you are an employer, you may wonder why workers behave this way. Many studies could answer that question, but a recurring theme is that workers give in to small office distractions because work hours are often the only time they have to socialize.

Both developed and developing countries face long commute problems. Urban housing is expensive in most big cities, so people settle for homes on the outskirts. The result is long commutes in and out of work. Far too many workers have to wake up early to reach work on time and return home late because of traffic. When work life looks like that, it is easy to give in to office distractions because that is the only way to relax and connect with others. Office chatter also helps mental health, which is why workers engage in it regularly.

Has anything been done about long commutes and long hours. Organized labor in some countries has pushed for shorter hours. In Germany, the metalworkers union IG Metall has secured 28 hour work weeks for 900,000 of its members. France is another example, famous for its 35 hour work week.

Still, these short work week policies are not widespread. Most workers around the world are still expected to spend eight hours in the office every day, and many of them must deal with long commutes on top of that.

A 2020 study of American workers, posted by Healthcareers.co, found that workers with higher incomes are more likely to experience stress on the job.

chapters.title: The four day week is not the gig economy
chapters.text:
When some business leaders try to push back on the four day week, they say it already exists and point to the gig economy. The two are not the same, so it is worth laying out the differences before going further.

The gig economy has roots in the United States. Before the late 2000s, the word gig referred to the contracts that musicians and artists booked. A jazz musician was said to have "gotten a gig" when they were booked to play a club, for example. Today, a gig can mean anything from freelancing on sites like Fiverr and Upwork to driving for Uber or renting out your home on Airbnb.

The term gig economy was coined in 2009 by Tina Brown, a former editor of The New Yorker.

Like the four day week, the gig economy promises flexibility and income, which is probably why people mix them up. The big difference is that gig workers lose every protection that comes with traditional work, while the four day week keeps all of those protections and adds more.

A gig worker is paid as long as they keep working. Once they stop or retire, there is no pension to fall back on. They are also on their own when it comes to health care and paid leave, which traditional workers enjoy. To make things harder, gig workers rarely get support to learn new skills. Freelancing on Upwork means you are on your own. The same goes for working for gig platforms like Lyft and Airbnb. Nobody is invested in making you a better professional. If that happens, it is up to you.

The four day week is not like that. It is traditional work with fewer hours, which means employers must follow standard employment laws covering 401K plans, vacations, and paid leave. Employers also contribute to local, regional, and national economies, unlike gig platforms, which often route work abroad to dodge taxes.

chapters.title: How to get your organization started
chapters.text:
This chapter walks through the important steps to run a successful trial and possibly roll out a four day week policy in your organization.

The first step is to present the idea to management. It can be hard to get buy in because this is still a fairly new policy. A solid way to convince leadership is with hard facts. Look at teams and employees inside the organization before you walk into a board meeting. Find out what your productivity data looks like. For example, you could run independent research and anonymous surveys asking employees how many hours they think they spend on real work each day. Compare your numbers with the UK and Canada research that suggested office workers average 1.5 to 2.5 hours of focused work per day. You will probably find that your workers are more productive than that, but can you show leadership that there is room to improve. Show them what improved productivity means for workers and for the organization, then ask for a trial.

Once you have sign off from the leaders, it is time to talk to a lawyer. You want to make sure the trial or the full rollout fits within the employment laws of your country or state.

The third step is engaging employees. This policy does not work without their direct involvement. CEOs like to think of themselves as sharp decision makers, and they are, that is how they climbed the ladder, but without the direct participation of employees, the four day week will not fly.

Employees need to understand that the free days off are a vote of confidence that they will deliver 100 percent of the expected results in only 80 percent of the time. Bring them into the process before the policy goes live. Make it clear that the 32 hour week is not a fixed arrangement. They do not have to work four days and take a day off. They could choose to shorten hours across five days instead.

Another step is to tell employees that they are free to flag time wasting factors in their workflow that could be hurting productivity.

Finally, if you start with a trial, analyze the results, ideally with help from an outside researcher, to see what kind of productivity gains or setbacks came from the new policy.

chapters.title: The four day week is for the greater good
chapters.text:
Let us zoom out and look at what a four day week can do for society as a whole.

Imagine a couple who both work for organizations with flexible hours. They could choose to have a baby without either having to pause their career. That single change would ease both inequality and low fertility rates at once. Spread that picture across cities around the world, and the scale of the benefits comes into view.

Flexible work also helps the environment. With fewer people in the office five days a week, there are fewer cars on the road, which means less traffic congestion and fewer carbon emissions. A New Zealand study found that productivity in the country could rise by about 9.4 million dollars each year if the road network were decongested.

On community impact, companies can ask workers to spend some of their free days on volunteer work. Perpetual Guardian did this with its employees. Part of its flexible work policy asks workers to spend one of their free days each quarter helping society in some way. With 240 employees, that adds up to nearly 1,000 days of community contribution each year. Imagine what would happen if larger companies asked their workers to do the same.

The four day week naturally nudges employees to give back to both their employers and the wider community for the free time they receive.

Finally, the four day week makes the world healthier. Having an extra day to relax or do what you love outside work cuts down on work related stress. According to FlexJobs, 80 percent of remote workers say they almost never feel work stress. If the four day week were more widely adopted, the number of de stressed workers could grow even more.

chapters.title: Do not miss the chance to improve lives
chapters.text:
Many business leaders lost millions in potential profit because they looked the wrong way while the internet was taking off. Only the visionary leaders who could see what the internet was becoming took advantage of it, and they are light years ahead of their colleagues today.

With every innovation, there are always late adopters, the group of skeptics who refuse to believe something can work until everyone else has already adopted it. Do not be the late adopter here. The age of flexible work has arrived. Give the four day week a fair try before you decide it will not work for your business.

The biggest internal obstacle to the four day week is intellectual resistance from business leaders.

Every business is different, so the approach you use may differ from what you read about or what others have done. The point is not to be rigid. You can adjust it as much as you like, as long as the core principles hold: reduced work hours at 100 percent pay in exchange for 100 percent productivity.

A flexibility or productivity policy is, at its heart, a test of leadership.

While running your trial, do not forget to design the office as well as you can. Open offices where employees can see each other and interact freely have their advantages, but without guardrails, they can hurt productivity. Research shows that when someone working on a complex task gets distracted, it takes roughly 40 minutes to fully regain the original level of focus. You do not want that happening in your open office, so put structures in place to prevent it.

Open offices should be tied to a clear goal, such as collaboration or spontaneous lunch meetings. If there is no goal or constraint attached to the open office, leaders are usually better off letting employees work behind closed doors.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
During the industrial revolution in the 19th century, workers were typically on the job 60 to 70 hours per week, or 3,000 or more hours a year. These workers were mostly men, and they had little to no time for anything else outside work.

Work culture was different then. You worked one job, usually for one company, your whole life. Job security existed, but work life balance was nothing to brag about, and the gender gap was wide.

That pattern held until labor got organized. People began pushing for better conditions for everyone. It was a long fight, but it led to the 40 hour workweek we now enjoy. So many Gen Z workers cannot even imagine working 60 to 70 hours a week. That is progress for humanity, and it is time to push further.

The four day week is the next stage of work. The principle can be compressed into the 100 80 100 formula, which means 100 percent pay for 80 percent of the time in return for 100 percent of the agreed productivity. The principle is employee focused and flexible in itself. When it is put in place, workers or a particular team can choose to take a full day off or to spread their hours across five shorter days. The choice is up to the workers, provided the company's bottom line is met or exceeded.

The four day week may look similar to the gig economy, but it is not. There is a clear difference between the two. The gig economy offers flexibility without the benefits that come with traditional work. The four day week is traditional work, with all its benefits, plus real flexibility. If you have to choose, pick the four day week, because your employer will offer you flexibility and will also be required by law to give you work leave, a 401K, and other benefits your region demands.

Try this. Your personal productivity affects any team you join. Here is a powerful tip to lift yours. Use time blocking. Block out large chunks of time where you focus only on work, without distractions. You can also set aside a short distraction window. Call it whatever you like, but the idea is to use that window for all the side distractions that usually interrupt your work. When it is work time, you focus only on work, and when it is distraction or break time, you do whatever you want. Keep that distraction window short.
